content.rendered:
Don’t be fooled by the title: Bruce Springsteen’s fourth studio album isn’t dark. It’s just real.

If Born to Run is unbridled romance, Darkness on the Edge of Town is unvarnished realism.

And that’s easy enough to understand: if you’d just settled a years-long lawsuit with your former manager that barred you from recording new music and forced you into a never-ending hardscrabble tour of secondary towns and tertiary venues, the romance would probably have been beaten out of you too.

Darkness on the Edge of Town is devoid of the romantic imagery and characters that populated Bruce’s previous album, and even the words themselves are tighter, terser, plainer. This is the album where Bruce transitioned away from his rhyming dictionary and vivid scenery and embraced an economical approach to storytelling that would become a trademark going forward.

Darkness is significant for more than just its lyrical evolution, however. It’s also the album on which the musical virtuosity of the “classic” line-up of the E Street Band can be heard and appreciated for the first time. Gone is the Spectorian wall of sound production, returning is Springsteen the guitar hero, and for the first time, Bruce’s voice is shaded with the empathy and world-weariness that comes with adulthood.

But don’t confuse realism with pessimism, because the unifying theme and greatest strength of Darkness on the Edge of Town is the relentless optimism, defiance, and persistence of its narrators.

Just as their author refused to compromise his principles and willingly paid the price for his resistance, the characters in Darkness refuse to submit to their lot in life. The resulting collection of anthems and ballads seem to seethe, mourn, and concede at first listen but rouse, galvanize, and uplift upon deeper inspection.

And deeper inspection is what we do around here.

Each essay in this book features early outtakes and work-in-progress performances, great live versions, and my own personal reading and consideration of each song. By no means do I consider these authoritative or representative of Bruce’s intentions, but they do reflect the results of deep listening, research, and reflection.

Let’s take a look inside the songs that comprise Bruce Springsteen’s fourth studio album, Darkness on the Edge of Town.

© July 5, 2022

excerpt.rendered: Bruce Springsteen’s fourth studio album becomes the first to receive the complete Roll of the Dice treatment.

aioseo_breadcrumb: Home » Roll of the Dice » Introduction

jetpack-related-posts.title: Darkness on the Edge of Town
jetpack-related-posts.author: Ken
jetpack-related-posts.excerpt: "Darkness on the Edge of Town" is one of Bruce's most enduring classics. But is it a song about heroic defiance or defeat and addiction?

jetpack-related-posts.title: Roll of the Dice: Texas Drive-In
jetpack-related-posts.author: Ken
jetpack-related-posts.excerpt: Among the lost tracks from a lost album is this teaser of a song about a secret love.

jetpack-related-posts.title: Vatican Cellars: Darkness on the Edge of Town
jetpack-related-posts.author: Ken
jetpack-related-posts.excerpt: Back in 2009, Vatican Cellars gave us a cover of "Darkness on the Edge of Town" both daring and beautiful--check out their cello-led arrangement inside.

jetpack-related-posts.title: Cover Me, Greg Kihn Band: Rendezvous
jetpack-related-posts.author: Ken
jetpack-related-posts.excerpt: When "Rendezvous" didn't make the cut for the Darkness album, Bruce went to Greg Kihn to make sure it found a good home.

jetpack-related-posts.title: Roll of the Dice: Sherry Darling
jetpack-related-posts.author: Ken
jetpack-related-posts.excerpt: "Sherry Darling" is synonymous with summer--one of Bruce's loosest, most carefree (and believe it or not, romantic) songs, yet it dates back to the serious Darkness sessions.

jetpack-related-posts.title: The Winter Blanket: Darkness on the Edge of Town
jetpack-related-posts.author: Ken
jetpack-related-posts.excerpt: The Winter Blanket gives us a quiet, introspective take of "Darkness on the Edge of Town" that conveys the resignation and defeat lurking at the heart of the song.
